name: Jowar Roti

hero.alt: Off-white sorghum flatbread on a wooden board with a thumbprint of ghee
hero.caption: jowar roti — patted, dry-cooked, ghee-finished

steps.title: Make a hot-water dough
steps.detail: Bring water to a boil with salt. Pour onto the jowar flour in a heatproof bowl, mix with a wooden spoon until cool enough to handle, then knead 3 minutes into a soft, smooth dough.
steps.tip: Boiling water gelatinizes the starch — that's what makes a gluten-free dough hold together.

steps.title: Pat into rounds
steps.detail: Take a lemon-sized ball of dough. Pat with damp hands on a piece of parchment or a plastic sheet, rotating in quarter-turns, until you have a round about 6 inches across and 3mm thick.
steps.tip: Don't try to roll with a pin — patting is the only way for jowar.

steps.title: Cook on a hot tawa
steps.detail: Slide the roti onto a hot tawa. Cook 1 min, flip, then move it onto an open flame for 5 seconds to puff. Smear a thumbprint of ghee on the hot side and stack covered to keep soft.
steps.tip: Don't press the roti while it cooks — the dough is delicate.

healthFacts: Sorghum (jowar) has a glycemic index around 50 — much lower than wheat or rice.
Naturally gluten-free; rich in magnesium, phosphorus, and B vitamins.
High in dietary fiber and resistant starch — supports gut health and stable glucose.
Polyphenols including 3-deoxyanthocyanidins are unique to sorghum among grains.
